quotes.verbatim_quote:
The word scale (another term that is used for the word scale is mode) refers to a’ group of notes
(tones) and the intervals or distances between any 2 adjacent ones.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Foundational definition of scale with 'mode' synonym.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Any 2 notes that are 2 frets apart(in other words, separated by 1 fret) are said to be a whole
step apart.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines whole step interval in fret terms.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Any 2 notes that are adjacent (right
next to each other) are 4 step apart.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines half step, completing two-interval vocabulary.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The major
scale has, starting from any note (which will coincide with the name of the scale), a series of two
whole steps, a 4 step, 3 whole steps, and anothet % step.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the W-W-H-W-W-W-H construction rule for any major scale.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The term TONE refers to the number of a note, if you were to number the notes of a major scale
from 1 to 8. For instance, D is the 4th tone of the A major scale. When talking about these tones, it is
customary just to say “D is the fourth of A”, or “B is the fifth of A” etc.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes numbered-tone shorthand for chord formulas.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Also the term “a third
higher” refers to counting 3 tones higher in the scale, including the tone you start from as the Ist
tone.

When referring to chords whose roots are based on a major scale, roman numerals are commonly
used. Example: D is called the IV of A; C¥m is the II minor (Im) of A; E7 is the V7 of A, etc,

Every chord has a certain combination of tones that distinguishes it from every other chord. These
tones may be derived from the major scale with the same root as the chord name.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core principle that chord construction derives from the major scale.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The
particular group of tones that is used for the construction of a particular kind of chord is called the
FORMULA of that chord; any chord that has 3 tones in the formula is called a TRIAD.

When tones are
ADDED to any of the 3 basic chords, the resulting chords are called EXTENSIONS. For example:
Cm7 is an extension of Cm because the bath (BD) is added to the basic Cm chord to make the Cm7. In
other words, 1, 63, 5, becomes 1, 63, 5,67, Any chord that has a STH, #5TH, DTH or #9TH in the
formula is called an ALTERED chord; any chord that has the 3RD replaced by a 4TH is called a
SUSPENDED chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Taxonomy of extension, altered, and suspended chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Any chord may be played with different arrangements of the tones in the formulas; these different
ways to play the same chord are called INVERSIONS or VOICINGS.

starting with a 3 or 4 note chord, RAISE each tone in the chord ON THE SAME STRING up
to the next higher tone IN THE CHORD.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Book's characteristic method for generating chord inversions.
